title-group.article-title: Prevalence of psoriatic arthritis in Chinese population with psoriasis: A multicenter study conducted by experienced rheumatologists

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Rheumatology and Immunology, Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200233, China
2Department of Dermatology, Ruijin Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine School of Medicine, Shanghai 200025, China
3Department of Dermatology, Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, Shanghai Institute of Dermatology, Shanghai 200040, China
4Department of Dermatology, Shanghai Skin Disease Hospital, Shanghai 200050, China
5Institute of Psoriasis of Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China
6Department of Dermatology, Changhai Hospital, Naval Medical University, Shanghai 200433, China
7Department of Dermatology, Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200233, China.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal. http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Reports on the prevalence of psoriatic arthritis (PsA) among Chinese patients with psoriasis are very limited. This study, conducted by rheumatologists, estimated the prevalence of PsA in a large number of Chinese patients with psoriasis.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: Consecutive patients with a confirmed diagnosis of psoriasis attending nine dermatology clinics in five hospitals were recruited. All psoriasis patients were asked to complete a questionnaire comprising 16 questions to identify possible cases of PsA. All patients with one or more positive answers to the questionnaire were evaluated by two experienced rheumatologists.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: A total of 2434 psoriasis patients, including 1561 males and 873 females, were enrolled. Both the questionnaire and rheumatologists' examinations were completed in the dermatology clinics. The results identified 252 patients with PsA, comprising 168 males and 84 females. The overall prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients was 10.4% (95% confidence interval [95% CI], 9.1%–11.7%). By sex, the prevalence was 10.8% (95% CI, 9.2%–12.5%) for males and 9.6% (95% CI, 7.7%–11.9%) for females and there was no significant sex difference in the prevalence of PsA (P = 0.38). Of the 252 PsA patients, 125 (49.6%, 95% CI, 41.3%–59.1%) were newly diagnosed by rheumatologists. Consequently, the prevalence of undiagnosed PsA among psoriasis patients was 5.2% (95% CI, 4.4%–6.2%).

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: The prevalence of PsA in the Chinese population with psoriasis is about 10.4%, which is almost double that of previous reports in the Chinese population, but lower than that in Caucasians.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) is a systemic inflammatory joint disease associated with psoriasis.[1] It is characterized by a wide spectrum of clinical presentations, including peripheral arthritis, axial involvement, enthesitis, and dactylitis, as well as skin and nail lesions.[1,2] In previous epidemiological studies, its prevalence in patients with psoriasis varied widely (6%–42%) among populations.[3,4] This discrepancy in PsA prevalence can be attributed to variations in study methodologies, particularly the diagnostic criteria (case definitions). The discrepancy is also partly due to differences in geographic locations and ethnicities.

The pooled prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients has been estimated to be 22.7% in Europe, 19.5% in North America, 21.5% in South America, 15.5% in Africa, and 14% in Asia.[3] Limited epidemiological studies in China showed a wide variance in the prevalence of PsA among Chinese psoriasis patients, from 0.69% to 7.14%, which is much lower than in most other Asian countries.[5] According to a recent meta-analysis, the pooled prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients in China is 4.9%.[3] The estimates in other Asian countries were 14.2% in Turkey, 13.5% in India, 8.3% in Japan, 35.5% in Thailand, 13.0% in Iran, 41.8% in Pakistan, and 10.4% in South Korea. Ethnic variations affect PsA prevalence, with the divergent distribution of human leukocyte antigen (HLA) possibly playing an important role.[2] However, the underdiagnosis of PsA in the previous epidemiological studies conducted by dermatologists in China may also contribute to an underestimation of PsA prevalence.

Most of the available data on PsA prevalence in China came from epidemiological studies aimed at estimating the prevalence and clinical characteristics of psoriasis rather than PsA.[6–8] All of these studies were conducted by dermatologists and only cases of established/advanced PsA were detected. However, the underdiagnosis of PsA in dermatology clinics is common worldwide.[9,10] Psoriasis patients with joint involvement but without a PsA diagnosis might be missed due to the absence of rheumatological evaluations. A cross-sectional study in Germany showed that, among 1115 psoriasis patients from dermatology clinics, 20.6% had PsA, with 85% of the PsA patients newly diagnosed after the rheumatological evaluation.[9] Similarly, a study indicated that, among 949 psoriasis patients from 34 dermatology centers in seven European and North American countries, 285 (30%) had PsA based on rheumatologists' assessment, with 41% of PsA patients not previously diagnosed with PsA.[10] Only one cross-sectional study has been conducted by dermatologists to evaluate the prevalence of PsA in a Chinese population of patients with psoriasis.[11] The results identified PsA in 5.8% of the Chinese patients with psoriasis according to Classification of Psoriatic Arthritis (CASPAR) criteria.[12] However, several factors might contribute to the underestimation of PsA prevalence, such as the high drop-out rate (21.7%) of participants, the method used to classify patients (some PsA patients might be misclassified as having other rheumatic disorders), and the single-center study design.

Therefore, this multicenter study was conducted by rheumatologists to estimate the prevalence of PsA among Chinese psoriasis patients attending dermatology clinics to provide new data on the epidemiology of PsA in the Chinese psoriasis population.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine Affiliated Sixth People's Hospital (Approval No. 2020-067) and all patients provided signed informed consent to participate in the study.

sec.title: Subjects and study design
sec.p: This study was carried out in February 2018 and January 2022. Consecutive patients with a confirmed diagnosis of psoriasis were recruited from nine dermatology clinics in five hospitals located in Shanghai. Patients aged <18 years and non-Chinese patients were excluded. A sample size analysis was conducted to calculate the statistical power. The sampling error was set as 0.01 and the confidence interval (CI) was set as 95%. According to a previous report,[11] the referenced prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients was estimated to be 5.8%. The sample size was thus calculated according to the following formula:

All of the psoriasis patients were asked to complete a questionnaire in the dermatology clinics to help to identify possible PsA patients. We recently conducted a study comparing the screening performances of the Early Arthritis for Psoriatic Patients (EARP) questionnaire, the Psoriatic Arthritis Screening and Evaluation (PASE) questionnaire, the Psoriasis and Arthritis Screening Questionnaire (PASQ), and the Psoriasis Epidemiology Screening Tool (PEST) in Chinese patients with psoriasis.[13] The EARP showed the highest performance in recognizing undiagnosed PsA. Furthermore, 12 key questions were retrieved from these four tools, including six questions from the EARP and another six questions from the PASE, PASQ, and PEST. To identify as many PsA patients as possible for further evaluation by rheumatologists, a questionnaire comprising 16 questions [Supplementary Table 1, http://links.lww.com/CM9/B547], namely, the EARP questions supplemented with six other key questions, was used to detect possible PsA patients. A positive response to a question was scored as 1, and the final score was calculated by summing the score of each question. For clarity, the modified questionnaire was referred to as the modified EARP (mEARP). All of the patients underwent a face-to-face initial evaluation by one of three rheumatologists (C.R., M.C., and H.Z.) in a dermatology clinic. During this evaluation, patients were checked for skin, joint, and nail changes and were invited to complete the necessary radiographic assessment, blood tests, and mEARP. The completeness of all of the completed questionnaires was checked at the site, and each patient with one or more positive answers to the questionnaire was evaluated by two of the above rheumatologists. The diagnosis of PsA was made by the agreement of the two rheumatologists. All of the PsA patients met CASPAR criteria.[12]

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Continuous variables are shown as mean±standard deviation or median (Q1, Q3). Categorical variables are shown as numbers and percentages. The Mann–Whitney U test or χ2 test was performed to compare the variables. Receiver operating characteristic curves (ROCs) with the area under the curve (AUC) were used to compare the diagnostic accuracy of the questionnaires. The optimal cut-off values were determined by the highest Youden index. A two-tailed P value <0.05 was considered statistically significant. R software (version 3.5.3, R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) and Stata (version 12.0, StataCorp, College Station, Texas, USA) were applied for all analyses.

sec.title: Prevalence of PsA
sec.p: The procedure of the present study is summarized in Figure 1. A total of 2434 patients with a confirmed diagnosis of psoriasis, including 1561 males and 873 females, were enrolled, comprising 703 patients from Huashan Hospital, 818 patients from Shanghai Skin Disease Hospital, 55 patients from Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital, 744 patients from Ruijin Hospital, and 114 patients from Changhai Hospital. The distribution of psoriasis patients by age and sex is shown in Supplementary Figure 1, http://links.lww.com/CM9/B547. The participants were normally distributed in different age groups. There were 828 patients with at least one positive response to the mEARP questionnaire who were further evaluated by rheumatologists in the dermatology clinics. After the rheumatological evaluation, 252 patients, including 168 males and 84 females, were found to have PsA. This gave an overall prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients of 10.4% (95% CI, 9.1%–11.7%). The prevalences were 10.8% (95% CI, 9.2%–12.5%) for males and 9.6% (95% CI, 7.7%–11.9%) for females, and there was no significant sex difference in the prevalence of PsA (P = 0.38) [Figure 2A]. The prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients by age group is shown in Figure 2B. Among patients under the age of 50 years, the prevalence of PsA increased with age (P for trend <0.01). In the 252 PsA patients, 127 (50.4%, 95% CI, 42.0%–60.0%) had been diagnosed with PsA previously; the other 125 patients (49.6%, 95% CI, 41.3%–59.1%) were newly diagnosed by the rheumatologists. Consequently, the prevalence of undiagnosed PsA among psoriasis patients was 5.2% (95% CI, 4.4%–6.2%).

caption.p: Study flow chart. PsA: Psoriatic arthritis.
Prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients by sex (A) and age (B). Data are shown as the prevalence with 95% CI. CI: Confidence interval; PsA: Psoriatic arthritis.

sec.title: Characteristics of PsA patients
sec.p: The age of onset of psoriasis peaked at 21–30 years, whereas that of PsA peaked at 31–50 years. There were 53% psoriasis patients with an age of onset ≤30 years, whereas only 26% of PsA patients had an age of onset ≤30 years and 47% had an age of onset of 31–50 years [Supplementary Figure 2, http://links.lww.com/CM9/B547]. The demographic and clinical characteristics of the subjects with and without PsA were compared [Table 1]. There were no significant differences in age, sex, age of onset of psoriasis, duration of psoriasis, type of psoriasis, and severity of plaque psoriasis between the patients with PsA and those without PsA. Plaque type was the major form in both PsA patients and cutaneous-only psoriasis (PsC) patients. The patients with PsA more frequently had a family history of psoriasis than the patients without PsA. Nail lesions, including pitting, onycholysis, and hyperkeratosis, were more common in patients with PsA than in those with PsC. The most common phenotype of arthritis was the oligoarticular type (44%), followed by the polyarticular type (36.1%). The occurrences of axial involvement and dactylitis in PsA patients were 19.0% and 39.3%, respectively. In addition, the prevalence of psoriatic comorbidities including uveitis and inflammatory bowel disease was significantly higher in PsA patients.

caption.p: Demographic and clinical characteristics of the subjects with and without PsA.

tr.td: Age of onset of psoriasis (years), median (Q1, Q3)

fn.p: BSA: Body surface area; DIP: Distal interphalangeal; IBD: Inflammatory bowel disease; PsA: Psoriatic arthritis; PsC: Cutaneous-only psoriasis; Q1: 1st quartile; Q3: 3rd quartile; SD: Standard deviation; –: Not applicable.

sec.p: The clinical features of patients with previously diagnosed PsA and newly diagnosed PsA were compared [Table 2]. There were no significant differences in age, sex, age of onset of psoriasis, and age of onset of arthritis between the PsA patients with a previous diagnosis and those with a new diagnosis. The duration from psoriasis to arthritis was also similar in the two subgroups, whereas the previously diagnosed PsA patients had both a significantly longer duration of psoriasis (14.3 years vs. 9.5 years) and a significantly longer duration of arthritis (7.0 years vs. 2.0 years) than the newly diagnosed PsA patients. The lag time to the diagnosis of PsA was significantly longer in previously diagnosed PsA patients than in newly diagnosed PsA patients (48.9 months vs. 24.0 months). Plaque was the most common type of psoriasis in both subgroups, and the severity of plaque psoriasis was equivalent in the two subgroups. The patients with previously diagnosed PsA had significantly more swollen joints compared with the patients with newly diagnosed PsA. The phenotype of joint involvement was significantly different in newly diagnosed PsA patients, with less polyarticular disease and more oligoarticular disease. The occurrences of axial involvement and dactylitis were similar for the previously diagnosed patients and the newly diagnosed patients.

caption.p: Clinical features of patients with previously diagnosed PsA and newly diagnosed PsA.

tr.td: Age of onset of psoriasis (years), median (Q1, Q3)
Age of onset of arthritis (years), median (Q1, Q3)
Duration from psoriasis to arthritis (years), median (Q1, Q3)
Delay to diagnosis of PsA (months), median (Q1, Q3)

fn.p: BSA: Body surface area; DIP: Distal interphalangeal; PsA: Psoriatic arthritis; Q1: 1st quartile; Q3: 3rd quartile; SD: Standard deviation.

sec.title: Performance of the questionnaires
sec.p: The responses of PsA patients to the mEARP questions are shown in Table 3, and the positive response rates among the previously diagnosed and newly diagnosed PsA patients were compared. The previously diagnosed PsA patients showed significantly higher positive response rates than newly diagnosed patients to several questions, including "Do your joints hurt?", "Have you ever thought that you might have arthritis?", "Has a doctor ever told you that you have arthritis?", and "Have you ever had a swollen joint?". Regarding the ability of the questionnaire to detect PsA patients among psoriasis patients, analysis showed that the AUCs of the mEARP were significantly higher than those of the EARP in both the previously diagnosed PsA patients and the newly diagnosed PsA patients [Figure 3]. The optimal cut-off values of the mEARP were four in the newly diagnosed PsA patients and five in the previously diagnosed PsA patients. Compared with the EARP, the mEARP had higher sensitivity and specificity to detect both newly diagnosed PsA (sensitivity, 89.6% vs. 80.8%; specificity, 97.9% vs. 97.3%) and previously diagnosed PsA (sensitivity, 99.2% vs. 94.5%; specificity, 98.4% vs. 97.3%) [Table 4].

caption.p: The 16 questions in the questionnaire and the positive rates of PsA responders.

tr.td: 2. Have you taken anti-inflammatory agents more than twice a week for joint pain in the last 3 months?
3. Do you wake up at night because of low back pain?
4. Do you feel stiffness in your hands for more than 30 min in the morning?
5. Do your wrists and fingers hurt?
6. Do your wrists and fingers swell?
7. Does one finger hurt and swell for more than 3 days?
9. Do your feet or ankles hurt?
10. Do your elbows or hips hurt?
11. Have you ever thought you might have arthritis?
12. Has a doctor ever told you that you have arthritis?
13. Have you ever had a swollen joint?
14. Have you ever had back trouble?
15. Has your back ever been stiff in the morning?
16. Have you had a finger or toe that was completely swollen or painful for no apparent reason?

caption.p: ROC curves and comparison of the AUC between the EARP and the mEARP. The AUCs of the EARP and mEARP were compared. A: Newly diagnosed PsA; B: Previously diagnosed PsA. AUC: Area under the curve; CI: Confidence interval; EARP: Early Arthritis for Psoriatic Patients: mEARP: Modified EARP; PsA: Psoriatic arthritis; ROC: Receiver operating characteristic; SE: Standard error.
Screening performance of the EARP and mEARP.

fn.p: CI: Confidence interval; EARP: Early Arthritis for Psoriatic Patients; mEARP: modified EARP; NLR: Negative likelihood ratio; NPV: Negative predictive value; PLR: Positive likelihood ratio; PPV: Positive predictive value; PsA: Psoriatic arthritis.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This is the first multicenter study conducted by experienced rheumatologists on the prevalence of PsA in a Chinese population with psoriasis. A mEARP questionnaire with 16 questions was used to screen possible PsA patients among 2434 psoriasis patients from nine dermatology clinics, and the diagnosis of PsA was made by experienced rheumatologists. The prevalence of PsA among psoriasis patients was estimated to be 10.4% (95% CI, 9.1%–11.7%); 49.6% of these PsA cases were newly diagnosed, leading to an overall prevalence of undiagnosed PsA of 5.2% (95% CI, 4.4%–6.2%) among patients with psoriasis. There was no significant sex difference in the prevalence of PsA.

Variability in PsA prevalence has been observed among different countries and even among different studies in the same country. For example, while one study reported that only 1% of Japanese psoriasis patients had PsA,[14] the estimates of PsA prevalence were 14.3% and 15.3% in another two studies of the Japanese psoriasis population.[15,16] This huge discrepancy in the prevalence was associated with the study design, for example, study objectives and researchers, participant recruitment, and the criteria used to diagnose PsA. Most studies of PsA prevalence were conducted by dermatologists. Although rheumatological evaluations were available in some studies, the referral of patients to rheumatologists may lead to participant drop-out. Because patients in dermatology clinics would tend to care more about their skin lesions and be unaware of the association between skin psoriasis and joint diseases, they would typically not be interested in undergoing further evaluation by a rheumatologist. Furthermore, there are challenges for dermatologists to select all of the possible PsA patients for referral to rheumatologists due to the complexity of the joint manifestations of PsA. These factors would contribute to underestimates of PsA prevalence in dermatologist-conducted studies. The study design and objectives also have an impact on the PsA prevalence results. Some data on PsA prevalence were retrieved from studies aiming at evaluating the prevalence or characteristics of psoriasis instead of the prevalence of PsA. The PsA cases in these studies were all previously diagnosed, whereas the undiagnosed PsA cases had not been detected, which resulted in an underestimation of PsA prevalence. In addition, misdiagnosis and misclassification of PsA also contribute to an inaccurate estimation of PsA prevalence. Underdiagnosis of PsA is common, especially in dermatology departments. According to a systematic review, the rate of undiagnosed PsA among patients with psoriasis at dermatology centers was 15.5%.[17] The remitting and relapsing course of PsA, wide spectrum in clinical presentations, and absence of uniform diagnostic criteria bring challenges to PsA diagnosis.[18,19] All of the above factors contribute to the varying reports on PsA prevalence.

The estimates of PsA prevalence in the present study were comparable to those in other Asian countries, but higher than that in a previous Chinese epidemiological study with a large sample of psoriasis patients.[11] That study was conducted by dermatologists in 2011 and reported the prevalence of PsA in psoriasis patients to be 5.8%. There were several causes of the low prevalence of PsA in the study. A relatively high proportion of participants (21.7%) dropped out and did not wish to undergo further dermatological or rheumatological evaluation, and they were eventually classified as non-PsA. Among the PsA cases, only 8% of them were previously diagnosed, which might be an underestimate. This rate is much lower than the rates reported in other studies, which generally exceeded 50%.[10,20–22] The single-center study design may have resulted in the participants not being representative of the general psoriasis population. In addition, patients with musculoskeletal symptoms were also directly classified as non-PsA in the study if they had been diagnosed with other rheumatic disorders without further rheumatological verification. Indeed, PsA is easily misdiagnosed by physicians without relevant experience.[23] Furthermore, some patients may have coincident PsA and other joint diseases, such as osteoarthritis and gout.[24] Therefore, these participants with joint involvement may be PsA patients but be missed. Taken together, these factors would contribute to an underestimation of PsA prevalence.

In the present study, there was also a possibility of missed diagnosis because it was not possible to evaluate all subjects by X-ray or MRI imaging of the spine/ultrasonic imaging of peripheral joints. To reduce the possibility of a missed diagnosis as much as possible, the most sensitive questionnaire, namely, the EARP, was selected from the four commonly used screening questionnaires.[13] Moreover, key questions retrieved from the other three questionnaires were added to the EARP, and this modified form of the EARP containing 16 questions, the mEARP, was used in the present study. All respondents who answered a single question positively were evaluated and diagnosed by rheumatologists. During the survey, the respondents filled in the questionnaire in the dermatology consultation room, and the rheumatologist helped to resolve any problems that the participants encountered in the questionnaire. The rheumatologist then checked the completeness of the questionnaire in the field and conducted a physical examination of the positive responders, which greatly improved the response rate. Compared with previous studies, this study has a greatly reduced possibility of a missed diagnosis. Of course, even this does not completely avoid such a possibility. In particular, completely asymptomatic PsA patients (especially those with pure axial involvement) may be missed.

On the other hand, if the researchers misclassified other arthritic or non-inflammatory musculoskeletal symptoms as PsA, the PsA prevalence might be overestimated. Musculoskeletal symptoms in patients with psoriasis are not sufficient to establish the diagnosis of PsA. Non-inflammatory symptoms should be differentiated from inflammatory musculoskeletal diseases, and inflammatory musculoskeletal diseases should be further differentiated from other types of arthritis, such as gout and osteoarthritis. This requires the investigators to have sufficient experience in rheumatology. In this study, the PsA patients were all diagnosed by experienced rheumatologists, which improved the diagnostic accuracy. Here, 36% of the newly diagnosed PsA patients were suspected to have arthritis by other physicians. This phenomenon does not mean that all suspected cases of arthritis in patients with psoriasis are truly PsA but that patients with suspected arthritis should be referred to a rheumatologist.

The participants in the present study were recruited from five dermatology centers. If the PsA prevalence in patients with psoriasis could be investigated in different regions across the country, the data obtained would be more reliable and representative, theoretically. However, there are numerous difficulties facing such a large-scale epidemiological survey, such as sufficient resources to launch a multi-regional study and difficulties in the standardization and quality control of the survey. Accordingly, this study was designed as a multicenter study to minimize deviation. Additionally, Shanghai is a city of migrants from all over China. The hospitals in which we conducted the study are all Grade III hospitals, with most patients from other regions. Because medical care in Shanghai is better than in many other regions of the country, both the prevalence of undiagnosed PsA and the lag time to establish the diagnosis of PsA may be less or shorter in Shanghai than in other regions with poor medical care.

In most PsA patients, cutaneous psoriasis preceded the joint involvement.[1] Thus, dermatologists are well placed to screen arthritis for an early diagnosis. Screening tools can help dermatologists to detect possible PsA in psoriasis patients, resulting in earlier referral for rheumatological evaluation and treatment. A well-designed screening questionnaire should have good sensitivity to recognize PsA and relatively high specificity to protect medical resources. In the present study, to identify as much PsA as possible, a screening questionnaire comprising the EARP and six other key questions was used. This new questionnaire showed more balanced sensitivity and specificity to identify both newly diagnosed PsA patients and previously diagnosed PsA patients. Nonetheless, this new questionnaire leaves much to be desired before it can be applied in practice. The content of the mEARP is more complicated than that of the EARP, and some items are not independent of each other. The inclusion of more questions in the questionnaire is not convenient for routine screening but helps to reduce misdiagnosis in epidemiological investigations. More importantly, the formation and validation of a questionnaire need to be conducted in individuals without an established diagnosis. A screening questionnaire is applied to identify undiagnosed PsA among psoriasis patients. If a questionnaire is developed in patients with previously diagnosed PsA, the determination of the cut-off would be disrupted, resulting in a poor functioning in early disease. For instance, previously diagnosed PsA patients who had already been treated would give fewer positive responses to the questionnaire, resulting in a lower cut-off point. In contrast, untreated previously diagnosed PsA patients would tend to give more positive responses, leading to a higher cut-off point. In the present study, the positive response rates to the questionnaire were generally higher among previously diagnosed PsA patients than among newly diagnosed PsA patients. Most of the published PsA screening questionnaires, including the PEST, PASE, and PASQ, were built using populations that included previously diagnosed patients. The cut-off values of these questionnaires obtained from the original studies are not applicable in clinical screening. Therefore, a new and improved screening questionnaire needs to be established and validated among undiagnosed cases in the future.

In conclusion, the prevalence of PsA in a Chinese population with psoriasis was estimated to be 10.4%, which is higher than that of all previous reports drawn from Chinese populations, but lower than that of reports drawn from Caucasian populations. About half of the PsA patients were undiagnosed before the study. If a screening tool is built from a cohort of previously diagnosed patients, the cut-off point of the screening tool will be higher, which will result in a lower sensitivity for the screening of undiagnosed patients.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82271837).

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: None.

fn.p: Hua Zhang and Miao Chen contributed equally to this work.
